title: The Kaveri Issue: Prime Minister Modi and Sonia Gandhi Must Intervene!
translatorNote: This translation carries Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recasting the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, chronological structure, quotations from court orders, figures and dates, figures and dates. English paragraphs follow the letter's natural units; the Tamil source's page-break fragments are merged. Udanpirappē, Kalaignar's characteristic address to DMK cadres and readers — literally "one born alongside me," joining the intimacy of "sibling" to the solidarity of "comrade" — is retained in transliteration. The original Tamil remains the authoritative text.
paragraphs: When the Kaveri river-water sharing case came up for hearing in the Supreme Court on 2 September 2016, the bench of Justices Dipak Misra and U. U. Lalit observed: "The Karnataka government should approach this matter humanely, beyond the letter of the law. A good climate must prevail between the states of Tamil Nadu and Karnataka. The Tribunal's final award states clearly how much water is to be given to Tamil Nadu each year, and how much is to be given in drought years and in years of scarcity. On that basis, the Karnataka government should deliberate on how much water it can give Tamil Nadu, and file it before this court" — and adjourned the case to 5 September 2016.

On 5 September 2016, having heard both sides, the Supreme Court judges ruled: "Considering the welfare of the farmers, for the cultivation of the samba crop, the Karnataka government shall release to Tamil Nadu fifteen thousand cusecs of water daily for ten days" — and adjourned the hearing to 16 September 2016.

The moment the Supreme Court's ruling came out, Karnataka Chief Minister Siddaramaiah said: "We cannot implement the release of water. Whatever is decided at the all-party meeting, the government will act accordingly." When the all-party meeting convened by the Karnataka Chief Minister began on 6 September 2016, the legal experts advised: "The Supreme Court's order must be respected and the water must certainly be released; failing that, the state will have to face the censure of the court." After the all-party meeting ended, Chief Minister Siddaramaiah announced: "By the Supreme Court's ruling, we are now compelled to release fifteen thousand cusecs of water to Tamil Nadu. Since everyone must respect the order of the Supreme Court, the all-party meeting has decided to release water to Tamil Nadu. We have taken this decision with a heavy heart." As the Supreme Court had ordered, at midnight on 6 September 2016 the Karnataka government released water to Tamil Nadu — ten thousand cusecs from the Krishnaraja Sagar dam and five thousand cusecs from the Kabini dam. Seeking a change in the Supreme Court's ruling that fifteen thousand cusecs be released to Tamil Nadu daily, and a stay of that order, the Karnataka government filed a petition in the Supreme Court on 11 September 2016. That petition came up for hearing on 12 September 2016. There, on Karnataka's behalf, it was argued: "Under the order the Supreme Court gave on 5 September 2016, we have so far released eighty-six thousand cusecs of water; we cannot release any more. That ruling should therefore be stayed." Having heard the arguments, the judges ruled: "Seeking to alter a court order by pleading agitations and unrest can never be accepted. Keeping the law-and-order situation under control is the state government's duty. Implementing the ruling is the state government's responsibility. The Karnataka government's petition is not acceptable. Until the 20th of this month, the Karnataka government shall release water to Tamil Nadu at twelve thousand cusecs daily" — and adjourned the hearing to 20 September 2016.

Meanwhile, following the Supreme Court's order, the Kaveri Supervisory Committee met on 19 September 2016 under the chairmanship of the Secretary of the Union Ministry of Water Resources, and at its close directed: "From the Kaveri, the Karnataka government shall release to Tamil Nadu three thousand cusecs daily from 21 September to 30 September." Neither the Tamil Nadu government nor the Karnataka government accepted that order.

In the Kaveri water-sharing issue, the Karnataka government goes on violating the Kaveri Tribunal's award without sparing a thought for the necessity of implementing it; and, in consequence, it looks straight past the misery of Tamil Nadu's farmers — the worst sufferers — with studied indifference. Now, instead of carrying out the Supreme Court's orders, it holds all-party meetings, cabinet meetings and special sittings of the legislature, passes resolutions, and manufactures a constitutional crisis and a politics of confrontation. That those who ought to be counselling the Karnataka government to abandon this approach — one that undermines the federal system — are instead lending support to the crisis-making course, is what gives pain.

At the hearing in the Supreme Court on 20 September 2016, the decision and direction of the Kaveri Supervisory Committee were filed as a report on the Union government's behalf. On Karnataka's behalf it was submitted: "With the water insufficient even to meet Karnataka's own drinking-water needs, water cannot be released to Tamil Nadu. Because of this, law and order in the state of Karnataka has been affected." On Tamil Nadu's behalf it was argued: "The Union government has not constituted the Kaveri Management Board as the Tribunal's final award requires. The Karnataka government must give Tamil Nadu the water that is its due." Having heard the arguments, the judges ordered: "The Union government shall constitute the Kaveri Management Board within four weeks. This dispute has existed since 1894 — for how many more days will you keep drawing it out? Until the 27th, Karnataka shall release six thousand cusecs of water daily" — and adjourned the hearing to 27 September 2016. This ruling drew fierce opposition on Karnataka's side, and a resolution was passed in the Karnataka Assembly. Thereupon the Karnataka government filed a petition in the Supreme Court contending that the order of the 20th could not be implemented, that no more water could henceforth be released to Tamil Nadu, and that the order of the 20th should therefore be revoked.

At the close of the hearing on 27 September 2016, the Supreme Court judges ordered: "Implementing the Supreme Court's orders is the duty of the Union and the state governments. To convene the Karnataka Legislative Assembly instead and pass a resolution cannot be accepted. In our country, which follows the federal principle, disputes between two states must be settled amicably, through talks. The Union government's part in this is important. The Karnataka government's action is contrary to the federal principle. This is a matter to be settled by talking. The Union government should call the Chief Ministers of Tamil Nadu and Karnataka and speak with them. For three days from 28 September 2016, Karnataka shall release six thousand cusecs of water daily to Tamil Nadu."

The Karnataka government has been manufacturing crisis and a politics of confrontation. That those who ought to be counselling it to abandon this approach — one that undermines the federal system — are instead lending support to the crisis-making course, is what gives pain. That ministers of the BJP ruling at the Centre — Ananth Kumar, Sadananda Gowda and Uma Bharti — have taken a stand against Tamil Nadu, and that former Prime Minister Deve Gowda has gone so far as to undertake a fast, are ill turns of fortune. Therefore, in this issue that has reached boiling point: since the responsibility for enforcing the Supreme Court's orders administratively rests with the Union government as well, Prime Minister Modi; and since, with Karnataka under Congress rule, a political duty falls to her as president of the All India Congress, Mrs. Sonia Gandhi — I urge that both intervene directly and come forward at once to explain the consequences and set the Karnataka government on the right course, with the aim of finding a solution, before the issue is turned aside from its course! With affection, M.K. 4 October 2016

provenance.translator: Translated from the OCR Tamil text, following docs/translation-style-guide.md and the model translations in docs/translation-models/
provenance.note: Retranslated July 2026 against the maintainer's translation models and approved v1-ch01 benchmark. English paragraphs follow the letter's natural units; the OCR page-break fragments of the Tamil source are merged in the English. Dates given in prose form (4 October 2016). மோழு in the OCR title read as மோடி per evident intent. The Tamil original is authoritative.
